notable.notable_blurb: Beaux-Arts architect of the New York Public Library

institutions.institution_blurb: Co-founder and partner of the Beaux-Arts firm Carrère & Hastings.
Designed Woolsey Hall and the Hewitt Quadrangle (1901).

cross_connections.display_label: commissioned his firm's first hotels in St. Augustine
commissioned building designs from his firm
lived with his family in the firm's townhouse during repairs

narrative_blocks.header: He designed the New York Public Library
narrative_blocks.body: With his partner John Carrère, Hastings designed the New York Public Library, regarded as a high point of American Beaux-Arts architecture.

narrative_blocks.header: His grandfather wrote the hymn 'Rock of Ages'
narrative_blocks.body: His grandfather, also named Thomas Hastings, composed the hymn 'Rock of Ages.' The architect carried the name into a different art.

narrative_blocks.header: He fought the rise of the skyscraper
narrative_blocks.body: Hastings denounced tall buildings as ruinous to the human-scaled city, urging that none be allowed to rise above eight stories, as in Paris.

narrative_blocks.header: He built the Indian Harbor estate at Greenwich
narrative_blocks.body: Hastings's firm designed Indian Harbor, the waterfront estate of Commodore E.C. Benedict on Long Island Sound. Benedict was the father of his wife, Helen.

narrative_blocks.header: He designed Arlington's Tomb of the Unknowns
narrative_blocks.body: After Carrère's death, Hastings designed the Arlington Memorial Amphitheater and the Tomb of the Unknowns, the marble sarcophagus honoring America's unidentified war dead.

cross_connections.display_label: was her father-in-law; President of Union Theological Seminary

narrative_blocks.header: She married the architect of the public library
narrative_blocks.body: Helen's husband, Thomas Hastings, designed the New York Public Library and the Frick mansion. Their 1900 wedding drew New York's wealthiest, with Stanford White decorating the church.

narrative_blocks.header: He led the seminary through a heresy trial
narrative_blocks.body: As president of Union Theological Seminary, Hastings presided over the trial of professor Charles Briggs, charged with heresy for questioning scripture's literal authority.

narrative_blocks.header: He admitted the seminary's first women students
narrative_blocks.body: During his presidency the seminary opened its curriculum to electives and admitted its first female students.

narrative_blocks.header: His father composed the hymn 'Rock of Ages'
narrative_blocks.body: His father, also named Thomas Hastings, was the church musician who wrote the melody for the hymn 'Rock of Ages.'

INST160.name: Carrère and Hastings
INST160.notes: Beaux-Arts firm of John Merven Carrère and Thomas Hastings (Hooker spouse, I01429), 1885–1929. Designed the New York Public Library, the Frick mansion, the U.S. Senate/House office buildings, Woolsey Hall and the Hewitt Quadrangle at Yale (INST022), and — for easter-egg Henry Flagler (X03079) — the Ponce de Leon Hotel, Whitehall, and the Standard Oil Building.

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.
